::: {.exercise}
Can you change the code to show the results for the
municipality of Rotterdam, ordered by party (starting from A)?
:::


```{webr}
#| setup: true
#| exercise: ex_filter
library(tidyverse)
votes <- read_csv("data/dutch_elections_2023.csv")
```

```{webr}
#| exercise: ex_filter
votes |>
  filter(municipality == "Amsterdam") |>
  arrange(desc(votes))
```

::: { .hint exercise="ex_filter"}
::: { .callout-tip title="Hint:"}

You can change the name of the municipality directly, as well as the column on which to sort.
What happens if you simply remove the `desc(..)` function, i.e. `arrange(*column*)` instead of `arrange(desc(*column*))`

```r
starwars |> filter(______)
```
:::
:::

::: {.solution exercise="ex_filter"}
::: { .callout-important icon=false title="🎓 Solution:"}

Change the column name for the ordering, as well as the name of the municipality. Drop 'desc' to default back to ascending order:

```{webr}
#| exercise: ex_filter
#| solution: true
votes |>
  filter(municipality == "Rotterdam") |>
  arrange(party)
```

:::
:::

```{webr}
#| exercise: ex_filter
#| check: true
gradethis::grade_this_code()
```


Do you know the biggest parties?
You can check the wiki pages for e.g. [PvdA/GL](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/GroenLinks%E2%80%93PvdA) and [PVV](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Party_for_Freedom) if you're curious -- the Netherlands has an extremely interesting and dynamic party landscape: Of the top-10 parties, more than half were founded in the last years or decades!
